title: Liberty Leading the People

image.license_note: Public domain. Faithful reproduction of a work by Eugène Delacroix (died 1863).

text.scene: A bare-breasted woman in Phrygian cap strides over a barricade of bodies, tricolour in one hand and a bayoneted musket in the other. Around her: a boy waving pistols, a workman with a sabre, a top-hatted bourgeois with a hunting gun, and Notre-Dame's towers in the smoke behind. The whole scene is smoke, dust and forward motion.
text.craft: Delacroix fuses reportage and allegory: dead men painted from the morgue share the canvas with Liberty herself, a goddess given dirt and body hair. The pyramid of figures climbs from corpses to flag, and the tricolour's red, white and blue is echoed and dirtied through the whole palette. The brushwork stays loose, urgent, almost reportorial.
text.painter: The great colourist of French Romanticism, Delacroix painted lions, massacres and Morocco, feuding politely with Ingres and the line-first classicists all the while. He witnessed the July Revolution but did not fight; painting this, he wrote to his brother, would serve as his form of service. France bought it in 1831, then hid it as too inflammatory.

fact: The revolution here is not 1789: it is the July Revolution of 1830, three days that toppled Charles X. The pistol-waving boy later fed the character of Gavroche in Les Misérables.
